prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. She wrote this the night before it was due. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10 (B+)

## What Works Really Well

- **Strong narrative arc**: Clear setup, failure, and reflection. The story is easy to follow and engaging.
- **Authentic voice**: This sounds like a real person, not someone trying too hard. The tone feels genuine.
- **Specific details**: "pick and roll," Marcus, the scrimmage, that kid saying "stay out of the way"—these ground the story.
- **Honest reflection**: Your friend doesn't just describe the failure; she actually extracts multiple layers of meaning (casual vs. competitive play, finding a better fit, self-awareness about knowledge gaps).
- **Strong ending**: "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" is a solid takeaway.

## What Could Be Stronger

- **Slightly under word count**: ~400-420 words vs. the ~500 target. Not a huge deal, but noticeable.
- **Tell vs. show**: Some sections summarize rather than recreate. The scrimmage scene especially could use more sensory detail or dialogue.
- **The debate pivot feels rushed**: It happens in one paragraph. Even a sentence or two about *why* debate clicked would strengthen this.
- **Very casual tone**: Phrases like "So yeah" and "obviously" work for voice, but an essay usually needs just slightly more formality.

## Bottom Line

This is genuinely good work for a last-minute essay. It does exactly what was asked—tells a specific failure story and reflects meaningfully on what was learned. The writing is clear, the self-awareness is real, and it's interesting to read. Your friend just needs slightly more detail, polish, and length next time. Great effort!

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Really Well

- **Strong narrative arc**: The essay has clear progression—overconfidence → humbling experience → specific failure → reflection → growth. It's easy to follow.
- **Honest self-awareness**: Your friend doesn't make excuses. She owns the overconfidence ("I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like") without being self-flagellating.
- **Specific, vivid details**: The "pick and roll," the kid saying "stay out of the way," the driveway games with Marcus—these concrete moments anchor the narrative and make it credible.

- **Authentic voice**: This reads like a real person reflecting, not someone trying to sound literary.

## Room for Improvement

- **Some telling instead of showing**: "I was really upset for a while" would be stronger if shown through an action or conversation rather than summarized.
- **The ending softens the stakes slightly**: "It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine" is a bit dismissive. She could sit with the disappointment longer before pivoting to the silver lining.
- **One or two transitions could be smoother**: The "Obviously" feels a touch sarcastic; "It turned out" or similar might land better.
- **Could go deeper on the debate pivot**: A concrete example of why debate worked would strengthen that claim.

## Bottom Line

This is a genuinely solid essay—especially for last-minute work. It hits the assignment squarely, shows real reflection, and has enough specific detail to feel earned rather than generic. The main thing holding it from a 9+ is that a few moments could be more vivid or introspective, but those are small tweaks.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. She wrote this the night before it was due. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
